evidence: The Océan class ironclads were a group of three wooden-hulled armored frigates built for the French Navy in the mid to late 1860s.
The ships were discarded during the 1890s.
The Océan-class ships had one horizontal return connecting rod compound steam engine driving a single propeller.
They carried 650 metric tons of coal which allowed them to steam for approximately 3,000 nautical miles.
Marengo participated in the French occupation of Tunisia in 1881 and was flagship of the Northern Squadron in 1891.
The Océan-class ironclads were designed by Henri Dupuy de Lôme as an improved version of the Provence-class ironclads.
Marengo's armament was upgraded, before she commissioned, to four 274-millimeter guns in the barbettes.
Océan was commissioned in 1870 to participate in the Franco-Prussian War and was assigned to the Baltic Squadron.
The ship measured 87.73 meters overall, with a beam of 17.52 meters.
Océan had a maximum draft of 9.09 meters and displaced 7,749 metric tons.
Tifon was an Uragan-class monitor built for the Imperial Russian Navy in the mid-1860s.
The ship was fitted with a two-cylinder, horizontal direct-acting steam engine built by the Baird Works of Saint Petersburg.
She was only active when the Gulf of Finland was not frozen, but very little is known about her service.
The French ironclad Thétis was a wooden-hulled armored corvette built for the French Navy in the late 1860s.
Thétis mounted her four 194-millimeter Modèle 1864 breech-loading guns in the central battery on the battery deck.
HMS Boreas was a B-class destroyer built for the Royal Navy around 1930.
She spent most of World War II on convoy escort duties in the English Channel and the North Atlantic.
The ship had an overall length of 323 feet, a beam of 32 feet 3 inches, and a draught of 12 feet 3 inches.
HMS Hostile was an H-class destroyer built for the Royal Navy in the 1930s.
She participated in the First Battle of Narvik in April 1940.
The ship had an overall length of 323 feet, a beam of 33 feet 10.1 inches, and a draught of 12 feet 5 inches.
The French ironclad Friedland was originally intended to be an iron-hulled version of Océan class armored frigate built for the French Navy during the 1870s.
She spent the bulk of her career assigned to the Mediterranean Squadron and supported the French occupation of Tunisia in 1881.
The German submarine U-41 was a Type IXA U-boat of Nazi Germany's Kriegsmarine that operated during World War II.
On 5 February 1940, U-41 was hit by depth charges from the British A class destroyer HMS Antelope after sinking two enemy merchant vessels.
The French ironclad Reine Blanche was a wooden-hulled armored corvette built for the French Navy in the late 1860s.
Reine Blanche mounted her four 194-millimeter Modèle 1864 breech-loading guns in the central battery on the battery deck.
HMS Imogen was a I-class destroyer built for the Royal Navy in the mid-1930s.
During the Spanish Civil War of 1936 – 1939, the ship enforced the arms blockade imposed by Britain and France on both sides.
The Graudenz class of light cruisers was a class of two ships built for the German Imperial Navy.
Both ships saw extensive service during World War I, primarily in the reconnaissance forces of the High Seas Fleet.
SMS Frithjof was the third vessel of the six-member Siegfried class of coastal defense ships built for the German Imperial Navy.
She served in the German fleet throughout the 1890s and was rebuilt in 1900 - 1902.
SMS Medusa was the seventh member of the ten-ship Gazelle class, built by the Imperial German Navy.
She served as a fleet scout in the period before World War I.
The Wiesbaden class of light cruisers was a class of ships built by the German Kaiserliche Marine shortly before the outbreak of World War I.
Wiesbaden saw only one major action, the Battle of Jutland, on 31 May – 1 June 1916.
The Wacht class was a pair of avisos built by the Imperial German Navy in the late-1880s.
Both ships served in the main German fleet for the entirety of their active duty careers.
HMS Swiftsure, originally known as Constitución, was the lead ship of the Swiftsure-class pre-dreadnought battleships.
She was transferred to the Mediterranean Fleet in 1909 and was sold for scrap in 1920.
Triomphante was the third and last ship of the La Galissonnière class of wooden-hulled, armored corvettes built for the French Navy during the 1870s.
The ship remained in Asia and never returned to France after the war.
HMS Shannon was a Minotaur-class armoured cruiser built for the Royal Navy in the mid-1900s.
She was paid off in 1919 and sold for scrap in 1922.
SMS Heimdall was the fourth vessel of the six-member Siegfried class of coastal defense ships built for the German Imperial Navy.
She served in the German fleet throughout the 1890s and was ultimately broken up for scrap in 1921.

evidence: The 20th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the New Zealand Military Forces, which served during the Second World War as part of the 2nd New Zealand Division.
It participated in the Battles of Greece and later in Crete.
It fought in the North African Campaign.
The battalion suffered heavy losses during Operation Crusader.
The regiment participated in the Italian Campaign, fighting in actions at Orsogna and later at Cassino.
The 2nd Parachute Brigade was an airborne forces brigade formed by the British Army during the Second World War.
Before the end of the Second World War in Europe the brigade saw active service in Italy, the South of France and Greece.

Impressed by the success of German airborne operations during the Battle of France in May 1940, the British Prime Minister, Winston Churchill, directed the War Office to investigate the possibility of creating a corps of 5,000 parachute troops.
The 26th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army.
It was assigned to the 7th Brigade and consisted of personnel recruited from the states of Queensland and Tasmania.
In the early part of World War II, the battalion was used in various garrison roles.
Later in the war, the entire 26th Battalion was committed to the Bougainville campaign.
The 23rd Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army.
It was raised in 1915 as part of the Australian Imperial Force for service during World War I.
The battalion was sent to Egypt to complete its training, before being committed to the Gallipoli Campaign as reinforcements in September 1915.
The 2/6th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army that served during the Second World War.
Raised in October 1939 as part of the all volunteer Second Australian Imperial Force.
The battalion took part in the first action of the war by Australian ground forces, the Battle of Bardia.
The 25th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the New Zealand Military Forces.
It was formed in New Zealand in 1940 and after a period of training, it embarked for the Middle East.
It then fought in the North African Campaign and suffered heavy losses during Operation Crusader.
The 2/31st Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army that served during World War II.
It was raised in June 1940 and was assigned to the 25th Brigade, 7th Division.
In late 1942 the battalion was sent to New Guinea to fight against the Japanese along the Kokoda Track.
The 2/15th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army that served during World War II.
Formed in May 1940 primarily from Queensland volunteers.
The battalion helped to hold the strategically important port, which had been surrounded following the landing of German troops at Tripoli.
The 15th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army.
It was formed in 1914 as part of the all-volunteer Australian Imperial Force from Queensland and Tasmanian recruits.
During the Second World War the 15th Battalion was mobilised for wartime service and initially undertook defensive duties in Australia.
The 14th/32nd Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army which served during the Second World War.
It was formed in September 1942 by the amalgamation of the 14th and 32nd Battalions.
The battalion was moved to Port Moresby, New Guinea, where they once again filled the role of garrison troops for almost a year.
The 2/17th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army.
Raised in April 1940 in New South Wales, it formed part of the 20th Brigade.
The battalion fought in New Guinea, conducting an amphibious landing as part of operations to capture Lae.
The 27th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army.
It was initially raised in 1915 as part of the First Australian Imperial Force for service during World War I.
During World War II the battalion was used mainly in a garrison role until the last year of the war when it was committed to the fighting against the Japanese during the Bougainville campaign.
The 28th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army.
It was raised in early 1915 as part of the First Australian Imperial Force for service during the First World War.
During the Second World War, the 28th undertook defensive duties in Australia for the majority of the conflict, before seeing action against the Japanese in the New Britain campaign.
The 6th Division was an infantry division of the Australian Army.
It was raised briefly in 1917 during World War I but was broken up to provide reinforcements before seeing action.
In New Guinea, its component brigades had a major role in the successful counter-offensive along the Kokoda Track.
The 2/9th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army during World War II.
Raised in Queensland as part of the Second Australian Imperial Force shortly after the outbreak of the war.
The battalion's final involvement in the war came during the Borneo campaign in mid-1945.
The 32nd Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army.
It was first raised in 1915 as part of the Australian Imperial Force.
During the Second World War the battalion was merged with the 14th Battalion to become the 14th/32nd Battalion.
The 2/10th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army that was raised for overseas service as part of the all volunteer Second Australian Imperial Force during World War II.
Formed in October 1939, it was the first 2nd AIF unit raised from the state of South Australia.
The battalion was committed to the fighting around Milne Bay, suffering heavy casualties in their debut in the Pacific.
The 2/5th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army that operated during World War II.
It was raised at Melbourne, Victoria, on 18 October 1939 as part of the Second Australian Imperial Force.
The 2/5th was one of only two Australian infantry battalions to fight against all of the major Axis powers during the war.
IX Corps was a corps of the United States Army.
Created following World War I, the corps was not activated for use until just before World War II.
The corps' only combat came in the Korean War.

evidence: The Australian weasel shark (Hemigaleus australiensis) is an uncommon species of ground shark in the family Hemigaleidae.
It is viviparous, with the developing embryos nourished through a placental connection and born after a six-month gestation period.
Feeding almost exclusively on octopuses and other cephalopods, the Australian weasel shark mostly hunts near the sea floor.
Thus, it has been listed as Least Concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN).
The sicklefin weasel shark (Hemigaleus microstoma) is an uncommon species of ground shark in the family Hemigaleidae.
Its reproductive mode is viviparous, in which the unborn young form a placental connection to their mother.
Given that fishing activity is intense throughout its range, the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has assessed this species as Vulnerable.
The banded houndshark (Triakis scyllium) is a species of houndshark, in the family Triakidae.
It is aplacental viviparous, with the developing embryos sustained by yolk.
Because fishing does not appear to have diminished this shark's population, the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has listed it under Least Concern.
The Izak catshark (Holohalaelurus regani) is a species of catshark, belonging to the family Scyliorhinidae.
This species is oviparous, with females producing encapsulated eggs two at a time year-round.
Despite the fishing pressure, its population has been increasing and thus the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has assessed it as Least Concern.
The hardnose shark (Carcharhinus macloti) is a species of requiem shark, in the family Carcharhinidae.
This species is viviparous, with the growing embryos sustained to term via a placental connection to their mother.
Given its low reproductive rate, the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has listed it as Near Threatened.
The thornback guitarfish (Platyrhinoidis triseriata) is a species of ray in the family Platyrhinidae.
It is aplacental viviparous, with the developing young drawing sustenance from a yolk sac.
The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has listed the thornback guitarfish under Least Concern.
The bigeye sand tiger (Odontaspis noronhai) is an extremely rare species of mackerel shark in the family Odontaspididae.
Reproduction is probably viviparous with oophagous embryos like in other mackerel shark species.
The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) cannot yet determine its conservation status.
The mangrove whipray or whitetail stingray (Himantura granulata) is a species of stingray in the family Dasyatidae.
It is aplacental viviparous, with the females nourishing their unborn young via histotroph ('uterine milk').
The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has assessed it as Near Threatened globally.
The cloudy catshark (Scyliorhinus torazame) is a common species of catshark, belonging to the family Scyliorhinidae.
It is oviparous, with females laying encapsulated eggs two at a time in nursery areas.
These activities do not appear to have negatively affected its population, leading it to be listed under Least Concern.
The sparsely-spotted stingaree, white-spotted stingaree, or Dixon's stingaree (Urolophus paucimaculatus) is a species of stingray in the family Urolophidae.
It is aplacental viviparous, with the mother provisioning her young with histotroph ('uterine milk').
The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has listed it under Least Concern.
The nervous shark (Carcharhinus cautus) is a species of requiem shark, and part of the family Carcharhinidae.
It is viviparous, with the developing embryos nourished through a placental connection.
The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) lacks sufficient data to assess the conservation status of this species.
The longtail butterfly ray (Gymnura poecilura) is a species of butterfly ray, family Gymnuridae.
This species gives birth to live young; the developing embryos are nourished first by yolk and later by histotroph ('uterine milk') supplied by its mother.
The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has listed this species as Near Threatened.
The blotchy swellshark, or Japanese swellshark, (Cephaloscyllium umbratile) is a common species of catshark, belonging to the family Scyliorhinidae.
This species is oviparous, with females laying encapsulated eggs two at a time.
The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) presently lacks sufficient information to assess its conservation status.
The Borneo shark (Carcharhinus borneensis) is a species of requiem shark, and part of the family Carcharhinidae.
The International Union for Conservation of Nature last assessed this species as Endangered.
The Borneo shark continues to merit conservation concern given its highly limited range within heavily fished waters.
The sharptooth houndshark, or spotted gully shark (Triakis megalopterus), is a species of houndshark, belonging to the family Triakidae.
This species is aplacental viviparous, meaning that the unborn young are sustained mainly by yolk.
The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has therefore listed this species as Near Threatened.
The peppered maskray or speckled maskray (Neotrygon picta) is a species of stingray in the family Dasyatidae.
It is viviparous, with litter sizes of one to three.
As a result, the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has assessed it as Least Concern.
The bramble shark (Echinorhinus brucus) is one of the two species of sharks in the family Echinorhinidae.
It is aplacental viviparous, with females producing litters of 15 – 52 pups.
The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) presently lacks enough information to assess the conservation status of this species.
The plain maskray or brown stingray (Neotrygon annotata) is a species of stingray in the family Dasyatidae.
It is viviparous, with females producing litters of one or two young that are nourished during gestation via histotroph ('uterine milk').
As it also has a limited distribution and low fecundity, the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has listed it as Near Threatened.
The deepwater stingray or giant stingaree (Plesiobatis daviesi) is a species of stingray and the sole member of the family Plesiobatidae.
It is probably aplacental viviparous, with the mother supplying her gestating young with histotroph ('uterine milk').
The International Union for Conservation of Nature has assessed it as Least Concern.
The leopard catshark (Poroderma pantherinum) is a species of catshark, and part of the family Scyliorhinidae.
Reproduction is oviparous and proceeds year-round.
The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has listed this species as Data Deficient.

evidence: Dragon Ball Z: Attack of the Saiyans is a video game based on the manga and anime series Dragon Ball for the Nintendo DS.
The game is a turn-based RPG that allows players to take on the role of six characters from the series.
It was released in Japan on April 29, 2009.
The game was released in Europe and North America in November 2009.
Super Mario World is a 1990 platform video game developed and published by Nintendo.
The game centers on the quest of Mario and Luigi to save Dinosaur Land from Bowser.
The game introduces new power-ups that augment character abilities.
It has been re-released four times.
Naruto: Clash of Ninja is a 3-D cel-shaded fighting game developed by Eighting.
It is based on the popular anime and manga series Naruto.
The objective is to reduce the opponent's health to zero using basic attacks and special techniques unique to each character.
Mortal Kombat: Deception is a fighting game developed and published by Midway.
It was released for the PlayStation 2 and Xbox in October 2004.
The game introduces two minigames that use MK characters.
The Konquest Mode returns from Deadly Alliance.
Marvel vs. Capcom 2: New Age of Heroes is a crossover fighting game developed and published by Capcom.
Players select a team of characters from the Marvel and Capcom universes.
The game uses similar tag team-based game mechanics to the series' previous iteration.
It features several significant changes, such as three-on-three gameplay.
X-Men Legends II: Rise of Apocalypse is an action role-playing game developed primarily by Raven Software.
It features the mutant supervillain Apocalypse as the primary antagonist.
The game was well received by gaming critics on all platforms.
Critics felt that the inclusion of online play and additional mutant powers made the game an improvement.
Dissidia Final Fantasy is a fighting game with action RPG elements developed and published by Square Enix.
The game features characters from different Final Fantasy games.
Players can customize their characters with equipment.
The aim is for the player to reduce their opponent's HP to zero.
Sonic & Knuckles is a platform video game developed by Sonic Team and published by Sega for the Sega Genesis.
It follows Sonic the Hedgehog and Knuckles the Echidna in their respective quests to save Angel Island.
The Sonic & Knuckles cartridge uses 'lock-on technology' that allows the game to connect to Sonic the Hedgehog 2 and Sonic the Hedgehog 3.
Critics were impressed with the lock-on technology.
Wonder Boy III: The Dragon's Trap is a platforming and action-adventure video game developed by Westone.
The game takes place immediately after the events of Wonder Boy in Monster Land.
It was praised for its colorful and cartoon-like graphics, rich sound and diverse sound effects, and varied and addictive gameplay.
The game won Electronic Gaming Monthly's 'Best Game of the Year' award for the Master System in 1989.
Super Mario 64 DS is a platform video game developed and published by Nintendo for the Nintendo DS handheld game console.
The plot centers on rescuing Princess Peach from Bowser.
The remake includes new characters, a multiplayer mode, and several minigames independent of the main adventure.
Overall, the game was critically and commercially successful.
Castle Crashers is a 2D beat 'em up video game developed by The Behemoth.
The game is set in a fictional medieval universe in which a dark wizard steals a mystical gem and captures four princesses.
Castle Crashers was well received by critics on all platforms.
The Xbox 360 version has sold over 2.6 million copies.
Mega Man 2 is an action-platform game developed and published by Capcom for the Nintendo Entertainment System.
The game continues the titular protagonist's battle against the evil Dr. Wily and his rogue robots.
The game features graphical and gameplay changes from the first Mega Man game.
Critics praised its audio, visuals and gameplay as an improvement over the first game.
Jetpac Refuelled is a shooter video game developed by Rare and published by Microsoft Studios.
The game follows Jetman as he attempts to rebuild his rocket in order to explore different planets.
The game is a remake of the original Jetpac with overhauled high-definition graphics and 128 levels.
It features competitive gameplay over Xbox Live, leaderboards, and achievements.
Adventures of Mana is an action role-playing video game developed and published by Square Enix.
The player takes the role of a young hero who, together with a heroine, tries to stop the Dark Lord of Glaive.
The gameplay focuses on sword combat, and is seen from a top-down perspective.
The game has received positive reviews, with reviewers giving particular praise to the visuals and the music.
Sword of Mana is an action role-playing game developed by Square Enix and Brownie Brown.
It is an enhanced remake of the original Game Boy game Final Fantasy Adventure.
The gameplay of Sword of Mana is an expanded and modified version of the gameplay of the action role-playing game Final Fantasy Adventure.
The player chooses to follow the story of either the unnamed hero or heroine.
Mario Kart DS is a go-kart racing game developed and published by Nintendo.
The game is the fifth installment in the Mario Kart series of video games.
The game features five single-player game modes: Grand Prix, Time Trial, Vs, Battle, and Mission.
Each course features item boxes that the player can drive through to receive a randomly selected item.
Guilty Gear Isuka is a 2D fighting video game developed by Arc System Works.
It was first released on December 17, 2003 in Japanese arcades.
The game introduced a four-player battle mode.
It features a 'turn' button, since a character does not automatically turn around if its opponent moves to the other side of the screen.
Blood Money is a side-scrolling shooter video game developed by DMA Design.
The game is set in four different locations on a planet, where players are tasked with attacking the enemies.
Players move through four stages on the planet, taking control of a different vehicle in each level.
Players can upgrade their vehicle with power-ups.
Blazing Lazers is a 1989 sci-fi shoot 'em up video game developed by Hudson Soft and Compile.
The gameplay features fast vertical scrolling and a wide array of weapons for the player to use.
Blazing Lazers was developed by the same personnel who developed other video game series.
It has received critical praise for its graphical capabilities, lack of slowdown, intense gameplay, and sound.

The game has 32 levels across eight worlds, followed by five bonus worlds.
The Lost Levels was first released in Japan for the Family Computer Disk System.
It was developed by Nintendo R & D4, the team led by Mario creator Shigeru Miyamoto.

evidence: The Kaivalya Upanishad is an ancient Sanskrit text and one of the minor Upanishads of Hinduism.
The text extols Shiva, aloneness and renunciation, describes the inner state of man in his personal spiritual journey detached from the world.
The text is notable for presenting Shaivism in Vedanta, discussing Atman (Soul, Self) and its relation to Brahman.
The Gospel According to John is one of the four canonical gospels in the New Testament.
John begins with the witness and affirmation of John the Baptist and concludes with the death, burial, resurrection, and post-resurrection appearances of Jesus.
The gospel is so closely related in style and content to the three surviving Johannine epistles that commentators treat the four books as a single corpus of Johannine literature.
The Tithonus poem is a poem by the archaic Greek poet Sappho.
The poem deals with the effects of ageing.
It is part of fragment 58 in Eva-Maria Voigt's edition of Sappho.
The poem has been partially known since 1922.
Du Friedefürst, Herr Jesu Christ, BWV 116 is a church cantata written by Johann Sebastian Bach in 1724.
The cantata is based upon Jakob Ebert's hymn 'Du Friedefürst, Herr Jesu Christ'.
The cantata matches the Sunday’s prescribed gospel reading, the Tribulation from the Gospel of Matthew.
Nyanyi Sunyi is a 1937 poetry collection by Amir Hamzah.
Analysis of Nyanyi Sunyi has focused on the theme of God and His relationship to humanity.
The collection has been noted as difficult to read owing to its usage of rarely used Malay and Javanese terms.
Gleichwie der Regen und Schnee vom Himmel fällt, BWV 18 is an early church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
The cantata is based on a text by Erdmann Neumeister published in 1711.
The prescribed readings for the Sunday were taken from the Second Epistle to the Corinthians and from the Gospel of Luke.
In Reformed theology, baptism is a sacrament signifying the baptized person's union with Christ.
Baptism also signifies regeneration and remission of sin.
Baptism is believed to be beneficial only to those who have faith in Christ.
In Christian theology, baptism with the Holy Spirit is distinguished from baptism with water.
It is frequently associated with incorporation into the Christian Church.
Different Christian denominations and traditions have interpreted its meaning in a variety of ways.
Wahrlich, wahrlich, ich sage euch, BWV 86 is a church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
The cantata is structured in six movements.
The prescribed readings for the Sunday were from the Epistle of James and from the Gospel of John.
Lobe den Herrn, meine Seele, BWV 143 is an early cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
The cantata is based on Psalm 146 and from Jakob Ebert's hymn.
The prescribed readings for the day were from the Epistle to the Galatians and from the Gospel of Luke.
The Devi Upanishad is one of the minor Upanishads of Hinduism and a text composed in Sanskrit.
The text describes the goddess as the highest principle, and the ultimate truth in the universe.
It is part of the five Atharva Shiras Upanishads important to Tantra and Shakta philosophy traditions.
Clement of Alexandria was a Christian theologian who taught at the Catechetical School of Alexandria.
He is regarded as a Church Father, like Origen.
Clement was influenced by Hellenistic philosophy to a greater extent than any other Christian thinker of his time.
The Gospel of the Hebrews is a syncretic Jewish-Christian gospel, the text of which is lost.
It is the only Jewish-Christian gospel which the Church Fathers referred to by name.
The fragments contain traditions of Jesus' pre-existence, incarnation, baptism, and probable temptation.
The Upanishads are a collection of texts that contain some of the central philosophical concepts of Hinduism.
The concepts of Brahman (Ultimate Reality) and Ātman (Soul, Self) are central ideas in all the Upanishads.
The Upanishads are the foundation of Hindu philosophical thought and its diverse traditions.
Buah Rindu is a 1941 poetry collection by Amir Hamzah.
The collection includes twenty-three titled poems and two untitled pieces.
The text is dominated by terms related to love and searching, and Amir's terminology is heavily influenced by classical Malay poetry.
Ach Gott, wie manches Herzeleid, BWV 58 is a church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
The text has been attributed to Christoph Birkmann.
The prescribed readings for the day are from the First Epistle of Peter and from the Gospel of Matthew.
Dynamic Science Stories was a pulp magazine which published two issues, dated February and April 1939.
The magazine only lasted two issues.
The contents were typical pulp science fiction, with few memorable stories.
Christ unser Herr zum Jordan kam, BWV 7, is a church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
The cantata is based on the seven stanzas of Martin Luther's hymn.
The topic of the cantata is baptism, which is treated based on biblical accounts.
Erforsche mich, Gott, und erfahre mein Herz, BWV 136 is a church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
The work is part of Bach's first annual cycle of cantatas.
The opening movement is based on a verse from Psalm 139; the closing chorale on a stanza from Johann Heermann's hymn.

evidence: The mathematical concept of a Hilbert space, named after David Hilbert, generalizes the notion of Euclidean space.
Hilbert spaces arise naturally and frequently in mathematics and physics, typically as infinite-dimensional function spaces.
They are indispensable tools in the theories of partial differential equations, quantum mechanics, Fourier analysis.
The success of Hilbert space methods ushered in a very fruitful era for functional analysis.
Integer sorting is the algorithmic problem of sorting a collection of data values by numeric keys.
Algorithms designed for integer sorting may also often be applied to sorting problems in which the keys are floating point numbers or text strings.
Integer sorting algorithms are usually designed to work in either the pointer machine or random access machine models of computing.
Different integer sorting algorithms make different assumptions, however, about whether integer multiplication is also allowed as a unit-time operation.
The impedance analogy is a method of representing a mechanical system by an analogous electrical system.
By converting to an electrical representation, these tools in the electrical domain can be directly applied to a mechanical system without modification.
The impedance analogy is widely used to model the behaviour of mechanical filters.
Another very common use is in the field of audio equipment, such as loudspeakers.
An antimetric electrical network is an electrical network that exhibits anti-symmetrical electrical properties.
References to symmetry and antimetry of a network usually refer to the input impedances of a two-port network when correctly terminated.
For an antimetric network, the two impedances must be the dual of each other with respect to some nominal impedance R0.
It is necessary for antimetry that the terminating impedances are also the dual of each other.
The mobility analogy, also called admittance analogy or Firestone analogy, is a method of representing a mechanical system by an analogous electrical system.
The mobility analogy preserves the topology of the mechanical system when transferred to the electrical domain.
The mobility analogy is widely used to model the behaviour of mechanical filters.
Transducers are provided at the input and output of the filter to convert between the electrical and mechanical domains.

DE 404 begins at the Maryland border, where it continues northwest into that state as MD 404.
There have been seven business routes of US Highway 31 in the state of Michigan.
Two of the current business routes, for Hart and Ludington, are business spurs.
Business US Highway 31 was a business spur running to the Niles downtown area along a former routing of US 31.
Delaware Route 30 is a state highway in Sussex County, Delaware.
The route runs from an intersection with Bi-State Boulevard.
DE 30 begins at an intersection with Bi-State Boulevard in southwestern Sussex County.
Delaware Route 4 is a state highway in New Castle County, Delaware.
The route runs from DE 279 and DE 896 in Newark east to DE 48 in downtown Wilmington.
DE 4 begins at an intersection with Elkton Road in Newark.
Delaware Route 71 is a state highway in New Castle County, Delaware.
The route loops off U.S. Route 13 from south of Townsend to Tybouts Corner.
DE 71 heads to the northwest from US 13 on two-lane undivided Summit Bridge Road.
Maryland Route 97 is a state highway in the U.S. state of Maryland.
The route runs 55.27 mi from U.S. Route 29 in Silver Spring north to the Pennsylvania border.
MD 97 is known as Georgia Avenue, which continues south from the southern terminus along US 29 into Washington, D.C.
Delaware Route 15 is a state highway in the U.S. state of Delaware.
The route runs from DE 14 west of Milford in Kent County to U.S. Route 301 in Summit Bridge.
DE 15 begins at an intersection with DE 14 west of the city of Milford.
Delaware Route 5 is a 19.48-mile-long state highway in Sussex County, Delaware.
The route runs north from River Road on the Indian River Bay in Oak Orchard.
DE 5 heads northwest on two-lane undivided Oak Orchard Road from the intersection with River Road.
Maryland Route 318 is a state highway in the U.S. state of Maryland.
The state highway runs 11.32 miles from MD 16 and MD 331 at Preston east to the Delaware state line.
MD 318 begins at an intersection with MD 16 and MD 331 at Linchester.
M-96 is a state trunkline highway in the US state of Michigan that runs between Kalamazoo and Marshall.
The highway now known as M-96 was originally part of M-17.

Delaware Route 10 is a state highway in Kent County, Delaware.
It runs from Maryland Route 287 at the Maryland border in Sandtown east to an interchange with DE 1.
DE 10 begins at the Maryland border west of Sandtown.
Delaware Route 16 is an east-west state highway in Delaware, mainly across northern Sussex County.
It runs from Maryland Route 16 at the Maryland border in Hickman to the Delaware Bay at Broadkill Beach.
DE 16 begins at the Maryland border in Hickman.
K-34 is a 29.26-mile-long state highway in southwestern Kansas.
It runs from U.S. Route 160 and US-183 near Ashland to US-400 near Bucklin.
K-34 begins at an intersection with US-160 and US-183 east of Ashland.
King's Highway 148 is a provincially maintained highway in Ontario, Canada.
The highway acts as an extension of Route 148 in Quebec.
Highway 148 connects Pembroke to the Quebec border at L'Isle-aux-Allumettes.
Maryland Route 291 is a state highway in the U.S. state of Maryland.
The state highway runs 18.34 miles from MD 20 in Chestertown east to the Delaware state line.
MD 291 begins at a roundabout with the eastern terminus of MD 20.
Delaware Route 3 is a state highway northeast of Wilmington in New Castle County, Delaware.
The route runs from an interchange at Interstate 495 in Edgemoor north to DE 92.
DE 3 begins at an intersection with Hay Road at an interchange with I-495.
M-19 is a state trunkline highway in the US state of Michigan.
The trunkline begins northeast of Detroit at a junction with Interstate 94.
M-19 begins at a junction with I-94 near New Haven at the interchange for exit 247.
There have been five different business routes of US Highway 23 in the state of Michigan.
These business routes were designated along former sections of US Highway 23.
Business US Highway 23 is a business loop of US 23 through downtown Ann Arbor.
Maryland Route 285 is a state highway in the U.S. state of Maryland.
The state highway runs 2.43 miles from MD 213 east to the Delaware state line.
MD 285 begins at an intersection with MD 213 just north of the Chesapeake City Bridge.

evidence: Mycena fonticola is a species of fungus in the Mycenaceae family.
The fruit body of the fungus has a smooth, violet-brown cap up to 2.5 cm in diameter.
The fungus was first collected by Japanese mycologist Haruki Takahashi in 1999.
The specific epithet fonticola is derived from Latin, and means 'dweller in fountain'.
Mycena clariviolacea is a mushroom in the Mycenaceae family.
Distinctive features of this species are found in its medium-sized, dark violet fruit bodies.
The mushroom was first collected by Japanese mycologist Haruki Takahashi in 2000.
The specific epithet is derived from the Latin words clari- (meaning 'clear') and violacea ('violaceous').
Mycena multiplicata is a species of mushroom in the Mycenaceae family.
The mushroom has a whitish cap that reaches up to 13 mm in diameter.
The mushroom was first collected by Japanese mycologist Haruki Takahashi in 1999.
The specific epithet is derived from the Latin word multiplicata, meaning 'multiplicative'.
Mycena lanuginosa is a species of mushroom in the Mycenaceae family.
The small mushroom is characterized by its grooved, grayish-brown to violet-brown cap.
Mycena lanuginosa was first collected by Haruki Takahashi in 2000.
The specific epithet is derived from the Latin word lanuginosa, meaning 'lanugineous'.
Mycena intersecta is a species of mushroom in the Mycenaceae family.
The mushrooms have olive-brown caps up to 12 mm in diameter.
The mushroom was first collected by Japanese mycologist Haruki Takahashi in 1999.
The specific epithet is from the Latin word intersecta or 'intersected'.
Mycena nidificata is a species of fungus in the Mycenaceae family.
The dark brown irregularly wrinkled cap measures up to 25 mm in diameter.
Mycena nidificata was first collected in 2000 by Japanese mycologist Haruki Takahashi.
The specific epithet is derived from the Latin word nidificata, meaning 'nidificate'.
Mycena fuscoaurantiaca is a species of mushroom in the Mycenaceae family.
The mushroom has a brownish-orange conical cap that has grooves extending to the center.
The mushroom was first collected by Japanese mycologist Haruki Takahashi in 1999.
The specific epithet is derived from the Latin words fusco- (meaning 'dark') and aurantiaca ('orange-yellow').
Amanita atkinsoniana is a species of fungus in the Amanitaceae family.
The fruit body is white to brownish, with caps that measure up to 12.5 cm in diameter.
The species was first described by American botanist William Chambers Coker in 1917.
The specific name honors George Atkinson.
Mycena mustea is a species of mushroom in the Mycenaceae family.
The mushroom's dull violet to grayish-violet cap reaches a diameter of up to 10 mm.
The species was first collected in Japan by Hiraku Takahashi in 1999.
The specific epithet is the Latin word mustea, meaning 'fresh'.
Mycena overholtsii produces some of the largest mushrooms of the genus Mycena.
They have caps that are 1.5 to 5 cm in diameter.
The species was first described by mycologists Alexander H. Smith and Wilhelm Solheim in 1953.
The specific epithet honors Lee Oras Overholts.
Amanita ravenelii is a species of fungus in the family Amanitaceae.
The fruit bodies are medium to large, with caps up to 17 cm wide.
The species was first described scientifically by Miles Joseph Berkeley and Moses Ashley Curtis in 1859.
The specific epithet ravenelli honors the American mycologist Henry William Ravenel.
Entoloma austroprunicolor is a species of agaric fungus in the family Entolomataceae.
The fruit bodies have reddish-purple caps measuring up to 5 cm in diameter.
The species was first formally described in 2007 by Australian mycologist Genevieve Gates.
The specific epithet austroprunicolor is derived from the Latin prefix austro-, meaning 'southern'.
Lactarius rufulus is a species of fungus in the Russulaceae family.
The fruit bodies have fleshy brownish-red caps up to 10 cm wide.
The species was first described by American mycologist Charles Horton Peck in 1907.
The type collections were made by A.M. Patterson and S. Nohara.
Mycena cinerella is an inedible species of mushroom in the family Mycenaceae.
The small grayish mushrooms have caps that are up to 1.5 cm wide.
First called Agaricus cinerellus by Finnish mycologist Petter Karsten in 1879.
The specific epithet cinerella means 'somewhat ashy color'.
Lactarius argillaceifolius is a species of fungus in the Russulaceae family.
The mushrooms produced by the fungus have convex to flattened drab lilac-colored caps.
The species was first described by American mycologists Lexemuel Ray Hesler and Alexander H. Smith in their 1979 monograph.
The type specimen is housed at the University of Michigan Herbarium.

evidence: NSB Class 64 is a class of three electric multiple units built by Strømmens Værksted for the Norwegian State Railways.
They were built for the opening of the Hardanger Line and served there until 1985.
The Hardanger Line opened in 1935 as a steep and curvy branch of the Bergen Line to connect Bergen to the Hardangerfjord.
Class 64 trains continued to be used periodically on the Flåm Line.
NSB Di 3 is a class of 35 diesel-electric locomotives built by NOHAB for the Norwegian State Railways.
The class was built between 1954 and 1969, and delivered in two series, Di 3a and Di 3b.
The class was the dominant locomotive on NSB's unelectrified lines.
It was ordered as part of the company's dieselization of services during the 1950s and 1960s.
NSB Class 66 was a three-car electric train used by the Norwegian State Railways for express trains on the Østfold Line.
The trains received the numbering BFM 66.01 – 04, B 66.31 – 34 and BS 66.61 – 64.
With a power output of 474 kW (636 hp), it was the first train capable of 120 kilometers per hour (75 mph) in Norway.
The class was retired from service in 1977.
T2000 was an electric train formerly used on the Oslo Metro of Oslo, Norway.
The T2000 operated on Line 1 of the T-bane, and replaced the aging HkB 600 teak wagons.
The T2000 was considered to be a limited trial.
If it met performance expectations, future orders could be made to replace the T1000 stock.
The NSB El 17 is a class of twelve electric locomotives built by Thyssen-Henschel and Norsk Elektrisk & Brown Boveri for the Norwegian State Railways.
The class was built in two batches, the first delivered in 1982 and numbered 2221 – 2226.
The traction system of the El 17 was based on the DB Class 120 of Germany and were among the first in the world to feature three-phase asynchronous motors.
The unreliability and lack of sufficient power forced NSB to instead use them in the regional Vestfold and Gjøvik Lines.
NSB Class 92 is a class of 15 diesel multiple units built by Duewag for the Norwegian State Railways.
The trains were refurbished in 2005 and 2006, and NSB plans to replace them by around 2019.
The two-car trains were delivered in 1984 and 1985, and put into service on the Røros Line and southern part of the Nordland Line.
Class 92 has considerably better curve speed and is able to operate at least at 80 km / h.
NSB Class 93 is a tilting two-carriage diesel multiple unit used by Norwegian State Railways for passenger trains.
Fifteen units were delivered between 2000 and 2002.
However, the technical problems to which the units have been prone and a cramped interior design have made them unpopular among riders.
The failure of the Di6, which were returned to the manufacturer Siemens, further motivated NSB to avoid locomotive-hauled trains.
T1000 and T1300 were two rapid transit train classes used on Oslo Metro in Oslo, Norway.
The first two single-car trains were delivered in 1959 and given the designation T.
In 1954, the Oslo City Council decided to build a four-line metro to the new suburbs.
The plans would lead to the system opening in 1966.
NSB El 18 is a class of 22 electric locomotives built by Adtranz and Swiss Locomotive and Machine Works for the Norwegian State Railways.
The class remains the only mainline electric locomotive used by NSB.
They have three-phase asynchronous motors with a maximum power output of 5,880 kilowatts.
They have a Bo'Bo' wheel arrangement and regenerative brakes.
Oslo Commuter Rail is a commuter rail centered in Oslo, Norway, connecting the capital to six counties in Eastern Norway.
The network spans eight routes and 128 stations, with Oslo Central Station as the central hub.
The system's predecessors date back to the opening of the Trunk Line in 1854.
Electrification started in 1922, and Class 62 EMUs were introduced in 1931.

evidence: The 1989 – 90 South-West Indian Ocean cyclone season was an average cyclone season, with nine named storms and five tropical cyclones.
The season officially ran from November 1, 1989, to April 30, 1990.
Cyclone Alibera killed 46 people and left widespread damage.
Severe Tropical Storm Ikonjo also had significant impact on land, when it left $1.5 million in damage.
The 1980 Atlantic hurricane season was tied with 1932, 1969, and 1994 for most named storms in Atlantic Ocean during the month of November.
The season officially began on June 1, 1980, and lasted until November 30, 1980.
Hurricane Allen was then the earliest Category 5 hurricane on record and also devastated portions of the Caribbean Sea, Mexico, and the United States.
Tropical Storm Hermine caused significant flooding in Mexico, which resulted in at least 38 fatalities.
The 1992 – 93 South-West Indian Ocean cyclone season lasted longer than normal, with the first storm Aviona forming on September 27 and the final storm Konita not dissipating until May 7.
It was an above-average season, with four tropical cyclones and six tropical storms.
Cyclone Colina formed and struck Réunion, which damaged houses and killed 2 people.
Ionia, the last of five system to cross Madagascar during the season, killed eight people in the country.
Hurricane Florence was a strong, late season hurricane that remained out over the open waters of the Central Atlantic for nearly a week.
With peak winds of 110 mph and a minimum pressure of 972 mbar, Florence was the strongest storm of the 1994 Atlantic hurricane season.
The origins of Florence can be traced back to an area of low pressure associated with a stalled frontal system located 1,150 mi east-southeast of Bermuda.
The storm intensified into a subtropical storm shortly thereafter before weakening to a depression the next day.
The 1960 Atlantic hurricane season was the least active season since 1952.
The season officially began on June 15, and lasted until November 15.
The most significant storm of the season was Hurricane Donna, which at the time was among the ten costliest United States hurricanes.
Overall, the tropical cyclones in 1960 caused at least 250 deaths and about $910.74 million in damage.
The 1998 – 99 South-West Indian Ocean cyclone season was a quiet season that had the fourth-lowest number of days with tropical storm or tropical cyclone activity.
There were only six tropical storms tracked by the Météo-France office.
Activity began late, with the first tropical storm – Alda – forming on January 16.
The strongest storm – Evrina – peaked as a strong cyclone in the Australian but weakened upon crossing 90 ° E.
The 1968 Atlantic hurricane season was one of five Atlantic hurricane seasons during the satellite era not to feature a major hurricane.
The season officially began on June 1 and lasted until November 30.
Hurricane Abby caused flooding and spawned four tornadoes, but left behind little damage.
Hurricane Gladys resulted in flooding across western Cuba, particularly in Havana, where the storm caused six deaths.
Tropical Storm Jerry was a short-lived tropical storm that formed in the latter half of the 2001 Atlantic hurricane season.
Jerry intensified into a tropical storm early the following day on October 7.
The origins of Tropical Storm Jerry can be traced back to a tropical wave on October 1, 2001.
The depression quickly strengthened into Tropical Storm Jerry, the sixth named storm of the season.
The 1981 Atlantic hurricane season officially began on June 1, 1981, and lasted until November 30, 1981.
The 1981 season was high in activity with 18 tropical depressions and twelve storms forming during the year.
Hurricane Dennis caused millions of dollars in damage in Dade County, Florida.
Tropical Depression Eight caused the most damage, due to flooding in Texas at the end of August.
The 1977 Atlantic hurricane season had the fewest tropical storms since the 1965 season.
The season officially began on June 1, and lasted until November 30.
Hurricane Anita struck Mexico as a Category 5 hurricane on the Saffir – Simpson hurricane wind scale, bringing strong winds and heavy rainfall.
Another notable tropical cyclone of this season was Hurricane Babe.
Tropical Storm Arthur was the first tropical cyclone of the 2002 Atlantic hurricane season.
It reached peak winds of 60 mph on July 16.
The origins of Tropical Storm Arthur are believed to have been from a decaying cold front in the Gulf of Mexico.
The system tracked quickly east-northward through much of its duration as a tropical cyclone.
The 1996 Atlantic hurricane season had the most major hurricanes since 1964.
The season officially began on June 1, 1996, and ended on November 30, 1996.
The most intense hurricane, Edouard, was a powerful Cape Verde-type hurricane that affected portions of the Mid-Atlantic states and New England.
Hurricane Fran made landfall in the same general area as a Category 3 hurricane, causing 37 deaths and $6.9 billion in damage.
The 1963 Atlantic hurricane season featured one of the deadliest tropical cyclones on record in the Atlantic basin: Hurricane Flora.
The season officially began on June 15 and lasted until November 15.
Hurricane Flora, which peaked as a Category 4 hurricane, dropped very heavy rainfall in the Greater Antilles.
Overall, the storms in this season caused at least 7,225 deaths and about $830.1 million in damage.
Tropical Storm Nicholas was a long-lived tropical storm in October and November of the 2003 Atlantic hurricane season.
Nicholas attained a peak intensity of 70 mph on October 17.
The origin of Tropical Storm Nicholas can be traced to a tropical wave that moved off the coast of Africa on October 9.
The depression moved to the west-northwest and gradually strengthened into Tropical Storm Nicholas.
Tropical Storm Norman was a tropical cyclone that hit Mexico in September 2000.
The storm produced heavy rain that resulted in flooding and mudslides, killing about nine people.
The origins of Tropical Storm Norman were from the same tropical wave that spawned Atlantic Hurricane Gordon.
The disturbance organized into a tropical depression on early on September 20.
The 2000 Atlantic hurricane season was the first Atlantic hurricane season without a tropical cyclone in the month of July since 1993.
It was slightly above average due to a La Niña weather pattern.
The most significant storm of the season was Hurricane Keith, which caused extensive damage in Central America.
Hurricane Gordon and Tropical Storm Helene both caused moderate damage in the Southeastern United States.
The 1973 Atlantic hurricane season was the first season to use the Saffir – Simpson hurricane scale.
The season produced 24 tropical and subtropical cyclones.
Hurricane Alice became the first known cyclone to affect Bermuda during July.
The most intense storm of the season was Hurricane Ellen, a Category 3 cyclone that remained over open water.
Tropical Storm Beryl was an unusual Atlantic tropical cyclone that formed over southeastern Louisiana in August 1988.
Beryl reached peak winds of 50 mph while located about 75 miles southeast of New Orleans.
The rainfall caused some reports of flooding, while prolonged durations of rough waves resulted in severe beach erosion.
Overall damage was minor, totaling about $3 million.
Tropical Storm Dottie was the ninth tropical cyclone and fourth named storm of the 1976 Atlantic hurricane season.
The storm drifted towards the east, and, after peaking as a moderate tropical storm, it accelerated northeastward and made landfall on Florida.
Damage from the storm was primarily insignificant and limited to gusty winds, heavy rainfall, and high tides.
A fishing boat capsized in the Bahamas, resulting in the deaths of four people.
The 2004 Atlantic hurricane season was the costliest Atlantic hurricane season on record until surpassed by the following year.
The season officially began on June 1, and ended on November 30.
Hurricane Charley made landfall in Florida as a Category 4 hurricane, causing $15.1 billion in damage.
The most significant tropical cyclone in terms of deaths was Hurricane Jeanne.

The Fifteenth Amendment prohibits the federal and state governments from denying a citizen the right to vote based on that citizen's race, color, or previous condition of servitude.
To enforce the amendment, Congress enacted the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
It was ratified on February 3, 1870, as the third and last of the Reconstruction Amendments.

Boehner decided to step down as speaker and resign his seat in Congress.

Kevin McCarthy, the House Majority Leader and second-in-command to the Speaker, was initially viewed as the favorite to win the Speakership.
The doctrine of legitimate expectation in Singapore protects both procedural and substantive rights.
To derogate from the representation may amount to an abuse of power or unfairness.

The recognition of substantive legitimate expectations is somewhat controversial as it requires a balancing of the requirements of fairness against the reasons for any change in the authority's policy.
the courts will grant judicial review of an administrative decision based on an individual's legitimate expectation if a public authority has made a representation.
The Colorado recall election of 2013 was a successful effort to recall two Democratic members of the Colorado Senate.
In the election, held on September 10, 2013, both Morse and Giron were recalled by the voters of their districts.
the Colorado legislature passed a series of gun control bills following the theater shooting in Aurora, Colorado.
The new laws provided for a ban on magazines holding more than fifteen rounds of ammunition.
The International Criminal Court investigation in Kenya or the situation in the Republic of Kenya is an on-going investigation by the International Criminal Court (ICC).
After failed attempts to conduct a criminal investigation of the key perpetrators in Kenya, the matter was referred to the International Criminal Court in The Hague.
The 2007–2008 Kenyan crisis followed the presidential election that was held on 27 December 2007.
A series of protests and demonstrations followed, and fighting — mainly along tribal lines — led to many deaths, injuries and displacements.
The Seventeenth Amendment established the popular election of United States Senators by the people of the states.
It also alters the procedure for filling vacancies in the Senate.
The amendment was proposed in the 62nd Congress in 1912 and became law in 1913 after being ratified by the required 36 state legislatures.
This amendment supersedes Article I, § 3, Clauses 1 and 2 of the Constitution.
The events surrounding the formation of the United Kingdom's government in 2010 took place between 7 May and 12 May 2010.
The election resulted in the first hung parliament in the UK in 36 years, sparking a series of negotiations.
The governing Labour administration led by Gordon Brown was defeated in the election and lost its overall majority.
The Liberal Democrats emerged from a meeting of their Parliamentary party to announce that the coalition deal had been 'approved overwhelmingly'.
The Old Court – New Court controversy was a 19th-century political controversy in the U.S. state of Kentucky.
The controversy began when the financial Panic of 1819 left many Kentuckians in debt.
A debt relief movement began in the state, and pro-relief candidates won majorities in the General Assembly in 1820.
The Assembly passed a law of replevin that was extremely favorable to debtors.
Singapore has a multi-party parliamentary system of representative democracy in which the President of Singapore is the head of state.
Parliamentary elections in Singapore are required to be held regularly to elect the Parliament through universal suffrage.
There are three separate branches of government: the legislature, executive and judiciary.
The Constitution vests the three branches of the state with different aspects of governmental power.
The Fourteenth Amendment addresses citizenship rights and equal protection of the laws.
The amendment was adopted on July 9, 1868, as one of the Reconstruction Amendments.
The amendment is one of the most litigated parts of the Constitution, forming the basis for landmark decisions such as Roe v. Wade and Brown v. Board of Education.
The Equal Protection Clause requires each state to provide equal protection under the law to all people within its jurisdiction.
Elections to Spain's legislature, the Cortes Generales, were held on 19 November 1933 for all 473 seats.
The right formed an electoral coalition, as was favoured by the new electoral system enacted earlier in the year.
The governing Republican-Socialist coalition had fallen apart, with the Radical Republican Party beginning to support a newly united political right.
The right capitalised on disenchantment with the government among Catholics and other conservatives.
The Parliament Act 1911 is an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom.
This Act must be construed as one with the Parliament Act 1949.
The Act effectively removed the right of the Lords to veto money bills completely.
It also reduced the maximum term of a parliament from seven years to five.
The United States Constitution contains several provisions regarding the law of criminal procedure.
Several of these rights regulate pre-trial procedure: access to a non-excessive bail, the right to indictment by a grand jury.

With the exception of the Grand Jury Clause of the Fifth Amendment, the Vicinage Clause of the Sixth Amendment, and (maybe) the Excessive Bail Clause of the Eighth Amendment, all of the criminal procedure provisions of the Bill of Rights have been incorporated to apply to the state governments.
If a defendant is convicted, the usual remedy for a violation of one of these provisions is reversal of the conviction or modification of the defendant's sentence.
The 1994 national convention of the Progress Party of Norway was held from 15 April to 17 April.
The party leader seat, held by Carl I. Hagen since 1978, was up for re-election.
The political disagreements roughly corresponded to a cleavage between two factions.
Both during the preceding months and on the opening day, resolutions were passed which ensured full dominance for Carl I. Hagen.
The Twenty-fifth Amendment deals with succession to the Presidency and establishes procedures for filling a vacancy in the office of the Vice President.
The Twenty-fifth Amendment was adopted on February 10, 1967.

Whenever the Vice President and a majority of either the principal officers of the executive departments transmit to the President pro tempore of the Senate and the Speaker of the House of Representatives their written declaration that the President is unable to discharge the powers and duties of his office.
The Chuck Baldwin presidential campaign of 2008 began April 10, 2008 as pastor and radio host Chuck Baldwin of Florida announced his candidacy for the Constitution Party presidential nomination.
Baldwin expressed paleo-conservative positions on a number of issues including protectionism on trade.
Baldwin left the Republican Party in 2004, believing it had become a 'second big-government, big-spending party.'
He joined the Constitution Party, but still considered himself a political independent.
